content.assessment: The proposal's hub-conversion mechanism explicitly cites Poland's 2025 draft legislation repurposing small-school infrastructure into social/cultural centers rather than binary closure (F-06), providing a direct structural template, though this finding is only weak-quality evidence and untested at scale. The lens also surfaces a genuinely contested empirical question relevant to the proposal's core rationale: whether preserving networked schools mitigates population decline. Polish village-level studies found smaller social-capital decline where schools were saved, but Finnish and Swedish studies are mixed or contradictory on the closure-depopulation link (F-07) — this contested cross-national evidence should not be resolved in favor of the proposal without caveat.
opportunities: Multi-service hub conversion could be sequenced ahead of full legislative mandate, using the Polish draft as a pilot template rather than a proven model
strengths: Poland's 2025 draft legislation offers a directly analogous repurposing template — treating rural schools as social/cultural centers rather than forcing a closure/non-closure binary (F-06)
Polish village-level evidence suggests preserving schools is associated with smaller structural social-capital decline, lending some support to the network-preservation rationale (F-07, contested)
threats: Given the contested cross-national evidence on closure-depopulation linkage, over-reliance on the Polish social-capital finding to justify network retention risks importing an unresolved causal claim without local verification (F-07)
weaknesses: The supporting Polish evidence (F-06) is explicitly weak-quality and describes draft legislation, not implemented/evaluated policy — reversibility and effectiveness are untested

The causal linkage between school preservation and community vitality is contested cross-nationally (Finnish and Swedish findings diverge from the Polish pattern), so portability of the rationale is uncertain (F-07)
